evidence: `goseg/protocol/contracts/protocol_contracts.go` and `goseg/protocol/contracts/startram_contracts.go` each define their own near-duplicate catalog constructor flow (`protocolActionContract` vs `startramContract`) instead of sharing one domain-level contract registration pattern.

The two files use different contract identity composition strategies (namespace+verb recomposition in one file, hard-coded `ContractID` in the other), which makes ownership boundaries and extension rules implicit rather than centralized.

StarTram-specific namespace primitives (`startramContractFamily`, `startramContractScope`) are declared separately from catalog entry construction and not integrated into contract identity assembly, signaling fragmented architectural intent.

Docker access is spread across multiple modules with separate client lifecycle helpers (`closeDockerSubsystemClient` in `goseg/docker/orchestration/subsystem/subsystem_events.go`, `closeDockerClient` in `goseg/docker/network/network.go`) instead of one shared infrastructure adapter.

High-fan-in runtime assembly (`buildRuntimeSeamBundle` in `goseg/docker/orchestration/runtime_ops.go`) binds config, docker network, registry, urbit, wireguard, and file IO into one dependency hub, increasing blast radius when infra dependencies change.

Workflow/import paths pull orchestration runtime bundles directly (`newWorkflowRuntime` in `goseg/shipworkflow/operations_runtime.go`, `defaultImporterRuntime` in `goseg/importer/importer.go`), which tightens package coupling to orchestration internals.
Boundary contracts are over-broad callback bags: `importerRuntime` and nested groups in `goseg/importer/importer.go` mix storage, auth, workflow, container, and transition concerns in one seam.

Startup handoff is similarly large (`startupInitRuntime`, `StartupRuntime`, and `startBackgroundServicesRuntime` in `goseg/startuporchestrator/startup_orchestrator.go`), which makes orchestration flow depend on many optional callbacks instead of cohesive collaborators.

Runtime composition in `goseg/shipworkflow/operations_runtime.go` embeds multiple orchestration op groups, so cross-module handoffs are implicit struct embedding rather than explicit narrow interfaces.

Several files carry compatibility or naming-shim indirection (`RunC2CCheckFn` + `RunC2cCheckFn` normalization in `goseg/main.go`, WiFi/Wifi alias methods in `goseg/system/wifi_runtime_api.go`) that adds control-flow and state normalization noise.

Periodic loop logic is duplicated with inconsistent mechanics (`runRoutineWithContext` uses `time.After` each iteration in `goseg/routines/system/system.go`, while other loops use tickers in `goseg/routines/logstream/logs.go` and `goseg/routines/version.go`).

Transition/event loops repeat similar worker supervision patterns (`startDockerSubsystemWithRuntime` in `goseg/docker/orchestration/subsystem/subsystem_events.go` and `StartVersionSubsystemWithContext` in `goseg/routines/version.go`) instead of a shared low-level primitive.
All four files contain only a concise package comment plus package declaration, with no verbose restating comments or pass-through wrapper code.
No debug-style logging, defensive overengineering, or repetitive boilerplate appears in the assigned files.
ai_generated_debt.issues_preventing_higher_score: The package comments are generic capability summaries and do not encode ownership boundaries or non-obvious constraints, which limits long-term maintainability signal.

evidence: No TODO/FIXME/deprecated markers are present in goseg/shipworkflow/doc.go, goseg/startram/doc.go, goseg/system/doc.go, or goseg/uploadsvc/doc.go.
No old/new API coexistence indicators appear in these files; they are documentation-only package stubs.
incomplete_migration.issues_preventing_higher_score: These files provide no explicit migration-status notes, deprecation policy references, or links to active migration tracking, so migration completeness is only weakly evidenced.

evidence: The scoped directories are all large and mixed-concern by structure signals: goseg/config/ has 41 files, goseg/system/ has 43 files, and goseg/shipworkflow/ has 45 files, which exceeds the >10 mixed-concern overload heuristic.

goseg/system/broadcast_info.go contains a broadcast-specific aggregation entrypoint (`CollectBroadcastSystemInfo`) inside the generic `system` package, indicating placement by historical convenience rather than ownership boundary.

goseg/config/bootstrap.go mixes bootstrap orchestration, filesystem path setup, runtime context initialization, and session-key lifecycle, while goseg/config/config.go also owns runtime context, startram cache state, and global mutable config holders; this blurs package-level boundaries.
Mixed acronym casing is active (not just historical): `RunC2CCheckFn` and `RunC2cCheckFn` coexist in `goseg/main.go`, with paired wrapper methods `runC2CCheck`/`runC2cCheck`.
WiFi naming also duplicates across the same public service type: `IsWiFiEnabled` + `IsWifiEnabled`, `ConnectToWiFi` + `ConnectToWifi` in `goseg/system/wifi_runtime_api.go`.
Most control flow in reviewed files is guard-clause based and explicit (for example runtime validation and early returns in `goseg/system/wifi_runtime_api.go` and `goseg/importer/importer.go`).
There is avoidable branch complexity from compatibility fallback paths (for example C2C/C2c selection in startup options), which adds cognitive overhead without core behavior changes.
logic_clarity.issues_preventing_higher_score: Compatibility branches and alias paths make flow harder to reason about than necessary, especially in startup/runtime wiring.

evidence: The reviewed runtime seams are strongly typed with concrete function signatures and explicit typed structs (for example `importerRuntime`, `RuntimeContainerOps`, `WiFiRuntimeService`).
Residual weak spots remain where dynamic payloads use `any` (for example event data and some log payload fields), reducing compile-time guarantees at boundaries.
type_safety.issues_preventing_higher_score: Dynamic `any`-style boundary payloads still allow shape drift and defer errors to runtime.

evidence: `GetStateJson` in `goseg/broadcast/state.go` always sets `AuthLevel` to `authorized` regardless of caller/session context.

Broadcast loop gating in `goseg/broadcast/loop.go` uses auth session presence (`HasAuthSession`) to control when broadcasts run, indicating auth state is meaningful but not represented in `GetStateJson` contract.
`goseg/importer/importer.go` relies on package globals (`uploadDir`, `tempDir`, `uploadSessions`) that are set in `Initialize`; upload handlers/pipeline logic consume these globals later.
`goseg/config/config.go` mutates runtime context in package `init()` and reads process args/env (`os.Args`, `GS_BASE_PATH`) during initialization, coupling behavior to process boot sequence.
Compatibility aliases are promoted as first-class API members instead of being isolated/deprecated (`StartC2CCheck` and `StartC2cCheck` both present in startup options; WiFi/Wifi dual methods).
Acronym and casing conventions diverge within the same packages (`EndpointURL` vs `EndpointUrl`, `ApiVersion` vs all-caps acronym forms in related names).

Public/runtime APIs expose dual entry points for the same behavior (C2C/C2c variants in `goseg/main.go` and `goseg/startuporchestrator/startup_orchestrator.go`; WiFi/Wifi method pairs in `goseg/system/wifi_runtime_api.go`).
Startup options maintain two callback fields for one responsibility and runtime fallback logic (`StartC2CCheck` then `StartC2cCheck`), which complicates integration contracts.

`setUploadResponseHeaders` in `goseg/importer/importer.go` sets `Access-Control-Allow-Origin: *` while also allowing `Authorization` and upload token headers, widening cross-origin credential surface.
The same module enforces token validation for upload session and chunk operations, but preflight/response policy does not align with that strict trust boundary.

findings.summary: Contract catalog construction is split into parallel per-file mini-patterns, weakening a single coherent ownership model for protocol contracts.
evidence: `protocol_contracts.go` builds IDs through `protocolActionContractID(namespace, action)` inside `protocolActionContract`, while `startram_contracts.go` bypasses that pattern and uses direct `ContractID` literals via `startramContract`.

Both files repeat bespoke wrapper constructors returning `contractCatalogEntry`, but with different required fields and identity semantics, so contributors must learn multiple registration idioms inside one package.
Architectural rationale for when to use namespace/action-derived IDs versus literal IDs is not encoded in a shared abstraction, making future contract additions prone to drift.
findings.suggestion: Introduce one package-level contract registration abstraction that standardizes identity construction and descriptor assembly across protocol families (including StarTram), then migrate both files to that shared pathway and encode the allowed ID strategy explicitly (e.g., typed family registry or builder with validated modes).

findings.summary: Cross-module seams are dominated by oversized callback bundles that obscure ownership and make handoffs non-local.
evidence: `importerRuntime` composes multiple nested runtime groups plus embedded orchestration ops; validation is group-based because the seam is too broad to reason about as one contract.
`StartupRuntime` and `startBackgroundServicesRuntime` contain large sets of independent callbacks, making startup sequencing rely on callback presence checks instead of explicit collaborators.
`workflowRuntime` embeds `RuntimeContainerOps`, `RuntimeUrbitOps`, and `RuntimeSnapshotOps`, so integration points are broad and implicit.
findings.suggestion: Split runtime bundles into capability-focused interfaces (for example: transition emitter, container lifecycle, urbit config, storage) and inject those collaborators explicitly per use-case. Add dedicated constructor types per workflow/startup path so each path receives only the dependencies it uses.

findings.summary: Docker dependency handling is fragmented across modules, duplicating client lifecycle and increasing coupling drift risk.
evidence: Both subsystem and network packages create docker clients directly and maintain separate close/error-join helpers.
Orchestration runtime composition selects concrete network runtime and other infra dependencies centrally, creating a broad dependency hub.
No single shared docker adapter contract is used consistently by subsystem/network orchestration paths.
findings.suggestion: Introduce a shared docker infrastructure adapter package that owns client creation/close/error semantics and expose narrow interfaces for event, network, and volume operations. Refactor subsystem and network modules to consume that adapter instead of duplicating client lifecycle code.

findings.summary: Compatibility aliases and mixed acronym variants add avoidable low-level branching and seam normalization.
evidence: `bootstrapRuntime` carries both `RunC2CCheckFn` and `RunC2cCheckFn` with fallback normalization logic.
WiFi service exposes duplicate method names (`IsWiFiEnabled`/`IsWifiEnabled`, `ConstructWiFiInfo`/`ConstructWifiInfo`, etc.) that duplicate API entrypoints and maintenance paths.
findings.suggestion: Define one canonical API spelling per operation and isolate backward-compatible aliases in a dedicated compatibility layer file with deprecation boundaries. Remove normalization branches from core runtime structs once call sites migrate.

findings.summary: Background loop/supervisor patterns are repeatedly hand-rolled with inconsistent timer and failure behavior.
evidence: `runRoutineWithContext` uses `time.After` inside an infinite loop while other routines use tickers, creating inconsistent scheduling mechanics and extra allocations.
Both version and docker subsystem start worker goroutines with near-identical waitgroup/error-channel orchestration logic.
Failure thresholds/retry semantics are implemented separately per loop subsystem rather than through one reusable supervisor primitive.
findings.suggestion: Create a shared routine supervisor utility (context-aware periodic executor + worker-group runner + retry policy hooks), then migrate system/version/logstream/subsystem loops to it for uniform timing, cancellation, and failure semantics.

findings.summary: Broadcast-state collector logic is placed in `system`, weakening directory ownership boundaries.
evidence: goseg/system/broadcast_info.go defines `CollectBroadcastSystemInfo`, explicitly tailored for broadcast state assembly rather than core host/system primitives.
The function directly consumes `SystemUpdates` (from apt flow) and `WifiInfoSnapshot`/SMART snapshots, effectively turning `system` into a broadcast aggregation layer.
This placement increases cross-domain change risk: broadcast payload evolution and system primitive evolution now share one package boundary.
findings.suggestion: Staged reorg plan: (1) create `goseg/broadcast/collectors/system_info.go` and move `CollectBroadcastSystemInfo` there while keeping primitive probes in `goseg/system`; (2) introduce a narrow `system.InfoProvider` interface in `goseg/system` and inject it into collector wiring; (3) remove direct reads of `SystemUpdates` from collector by exposing a getter/service; (4) update imports with `rg "CollectBroadcastSystemInfo|SystemUpdates" goseg -n` and `go test ./goseg/system ./goseg/broadcast/...`; (5) run `go test ./...` for full validation.

findings.summary: The `config` package is a flat, overloaded directory with multiple responsibilities in one boundary.
evidence: Structure profile shows `goseg/config/` contains 41 files, indicating a broad mixed-concern surface.
bootstrap.go handles initialization workflow, disk path bootstrap, key-file provisioning, and runtime merge/persist behavior.
config.go concurrently owns runtime context defaults, mutable global holders, and startram snapshot/cache state, indicating orthogonal concerns grouped by convenience.
findings.suggestion: Staged reorg plan: (1) create subfolders `goseg/config/runtime/`, `goseg/config/bootstrap/`, and `goseg/config/startramstate/`; (2) first move runtime-context types/functions from config.go into `runtime/` and update call sites; (3) second move initialization pipeline from bootstrap.go into `bootstrap/` with exported façade methods in `config`; (4) third isolate startram cache/snapshot state into `startramstate/`; (5) update imports via `goimports ./goseg/config/...` and verify with `go test ./goseg/config/... && go test ./...`.

findings.summary: Subsystems mix explicit seam-injection and package-global mutable state, reducing architectural coherence.
evidence: shipworkflow/lifecycle.go models dependencies through function-typed seams (`shipStatusLookup`, `shipConfigPersist`, `pollerFunc`) and avoids package-global reads.
system/apt.go and config/bootstrap.go+config.go depend on package-global mutable state (`SystemUpdates`, `globalConfig`, `Ready`, `initOnce`) as primary architecture.
This creates unpredictable ownership and change boundaries: similar lifecycle concerns are solved with incompatible architectural patterns in adjacent domains.
findings.suggestion: Adopt a single boundary style in stages: (1) define small service structs for config bootstrap and system update state with explicit dependencies; (2) keep package-level wrappers temporarily for compatibility, delegating to injectable services; (3) migrate callers incrementally (`shipworkflow` style seams for new paths first), then remove direct global access; (4) validate each stage with `go test ./goseg/config/... ./goseg/system/... ./goseg/shipworkflow/...` and final `go test ./...`.

findings.summary: Governance policy is documented as architectural contract but not enforced by shared catalog construction.
evidence: README.md requires compatibility descriptors and namespace/action contract discipline across active families.
protocol_contracts.go and startram_contracts.go build descriptors independently with different patterns and no shared policy validator in these files.
startram_contracts.go defines family/scope constants that are unused, indicating intended governance structure is not part of runtime construction.
findings.suggestion: Introduce a single policy-enforcing constructor path for family catalogs and migrate both files to it in stages: Stage 1 create `goseg/protocol/contracts/families/shared` (or equivalent) with a typed family descriptor/builder that requires family, scope, metadata, and lifecycle fields; Stage 2 move StarTram and protocol action catalog specs under family folders (`goseg/protocol/contracts/families/startram/` and `.../families/protocol/`) and have each register through the shared builder; Stage 3 update imports/call sites and run `cd goseg && go test ./protocol/contracts ./protocol/actions ./startram` plus `go test ./...` to validate unchanged contract IDs and metadata semantics.

findings.summary: Single contracts package blends cross-family kernel code with per-family catalogs, blurring ownership boundaries.
evidence: Directory profile shows `goseg/protocol/contracts/` as the sole location for both shared governance references and per-family catalog definitions.
README.md’s governance section treats family contract surfaces as explicit policy-controlled areas, but package placement keeps all concerns flattened under one folder.
The two family files already diverge in internal conventions (namespace-derived vs explicit ID construction), which is harder to normalize when shared and family responsibilities are colocated.
findings.suggestion: Apply a staged reorg: Stage 1 create target folders `goseg/protocol/contracts/core/` (shared IDs/metadata/registry) and `goseg/protocol/contracts/families/{protocol,startram}/` (family catalogs); Stage 2 move `protocol_contracts.go` and `startram_contracts.go` into their family folders, keep thin compatibility shims in original paths temporarily if needed; Stage 3 update package imports and registration wiring, then remove shims after callers are updated. Validate with `cd goseg && go test ./protocol/contracts ./protocol/actions ./startram` and then `go test ./...`.

findings.summary: Compatibility aliases are exposed as parallel public APIs, creating duplicate integration paths for the same behavior.
evidence: `bootstrapRuntime` defines both `RunC2CCheckFn` and `RunC2cCheckFn`, and startup options expose both `StartC2CCheck` and `StartC2cCheck` with fallback wiring.
WiFi runtime service exports duplicate method pairs (`IsWiFiEnabled`/`IsWifiEnabled`, `ConnectToWiFi`/`ConnectToWifi`) rather than one canonical surface.
findings.suggestion: Define one canonical exported API per behavior (prefer consistent acronym casing), keep aliases internal-only with explicit deprecation shims, and remove dual callback fields from startup options after migration.

findings.summary: Inconsistent acronym casing (`C2C`/`C2c`, `WiFi`/`Wifi`, `API`/`Api`) obscures canonical domain vocabulary.
evidence: Both `defaultC2CRuntimeContext` and `defaultC2cRuntimeContext` exist and are used.
`ErrWiFiInterfaceNotFound` and `ErrWifiInterfaceNotFound` both exported; similar dual naming pattern appears across WiFi methods.
Config fields use `EndpointUrl`/`ApiVersion` while related code uses all-caps acronym forms (`EndpointURL`, `APIConnectionErrorMessage`).
findings.suggestion: Adopt one acronym/casing standard for exported identifiers and serialized field naming policy, then codemod call sites and keep temporary aliases only behind a deprecation window.

findings.summary: Broadcast state serialization hardcodes authorized auth level, decoupling payload contract from actual auth/session state.
evidence: `GetStateJson` always emits `AuthLevel: authorized` regardless of runtime context.
`runBroadcastTickWithRuntime` uses `HasAuthSession` as a gate for broadcast activity, indicating auth state is dynamic and meaningful.
Tests currently lock in the hardcoded behavior (`expected auth_level=authorized`).
findings.suggestion: Pass auth context into serialization (or derive it from the session manager at call boundary), emit correct auth levels, and update tests to validate context-sensitive auth-level behavior.

findings.summary: Upload endpoints enforce token auth but expose permissive wildcard CORS headers that weaken the intended trust boundary.
evidence: `setUploadResponseHeaders` sets `Access-Control-Allow-Origin` to `*` and allows `Authorization`, `X-Upload-Token-Id`, and `X-Upload-Token` headers.
Upload flow performs strict token/session checks (`validateUploadRequest`, `OpenUploadEndpoint`), but origin policy does not reflect equivalent strictness.
findings.suggestion: Restrict allowed origins to configured trusted UI origins, explicitly control credentialed requests, and enforce a single policy helper for all authenticated upload endpoints.

findings.summary: Core runtime state depends on package-level globals initialized in specific startup order, coupling handlers to bootstrap sequencing.
evidence: Importer uses global mutable state (`uploadDir`, `tempDir`, `uploadSessions`) populated in `Initialize`; pipeline code later depends on that ambient state.
Startup orchestrator explicitly includes `InitializeImporterFn` in boot sequence, indicating order-sensitive setup.
Config package sets runtime context in `init()` and reads process inputs during initialization, further binding behavior to process start state.
findings.suggestion: Replace package-global mutable runtime state with an explicit injected runtime/service instance passed through startup wiring and HTTP handler registration; minimize `init()` behavior to immutable constants.

findings.summary: Backward-compatibility aliases are treated as persistent primary conventions, creating style islands and migration stall.
evidence: Alias members include comments like compatibility retention but remain in active runtime resolution paths.
Tests and runtime options continue to use alias names, so migration never converges on one convention.
findings.suggestion: Define a timed migration policy: canonical names only in new code, aliases behind deprecation wrappers, and cleanup tickets to remove alias code paths after call-site migration.
